definition: Lenovo ThinkStation P330 Tiny. CPU: Intel i7-8700 (6-core, 12-thread, 3.2GHz base / 4.6GHz boost). RAM: 32GB DDR4. OS: Windows 10 LTSC. Form factor: compact desktop (1L Tiny). Thermal management via ThrottleStop: PL1=40W, PL2=35W. Sustained CPU temps: 65-77C under inference load.

Power limits set via ThrottleStop to prevent thermal throttle on P330 Tiny. PL1=40W (sustained), PL2=35W (burst). Counterintuitive: PL2 < PL1 is intentional -- flattens power curve, prevents brief spikes that trigger throttle. Result: sustained temps 65-77C, stable tok/s across long sessions.

Current GPU in Bobby's stack. NVIDIA Quadro P1000, Pascal architecture (GP107), 14nm. 4GB GDDR5 VRAM, 128-bit bus, 82 GB/s bandwidth. 640 CUDA cores. Base 1266MHz, boost 1480MHz. Low-profile form factor -- fits P330 Tiny. Effective available VRAM at llama.cpp launch: ~3.8GB (OS overhead eats ~200MB). TDP: 47W. Consumer equivalent: roughly GTX 1050 class.

Effective VRAM at llama.cpp launch: ~3.8GB. Practical limits for Hivemind 4B: Q4_K_M fits comfortably (~2.5-2.8GB weights + KV cache headroom at ctx 8192). Q5_K_M: tight, may not fit with full ctx. Q8_0: too large. Rule: Q4_K_M is the validated sweet spot on P1000. Always check VRAM usage after load -- if model spills to CPU RAM, tok/s drops dramatically. # TODO: validate exact VRAM figures post P4 install comparison

Hivemind 4B at Q4_K_M: 13-16 tok/s on P1000. This is the validated baseline. Phi-4-mini: faster due to smaller parameter count. Swarm models (0.6B-1.7B workers): significantly faster, not the bottleneck. The P1000 is bandwidth-limited -- 96 GB/s is the ceiling. All models in the stack are running close to that ceiling at these sizes.

Quadro P1000 is roughly equivalent to a GTX 1050 (desktop) in raw compute -- same GP107 die, similar CUDA core count and clocks. If someone asks 'what GPU is that like', GTX 1050 is the honest answer. Not a gaming GPU recommendation -- just the closest consumer reference point for context.

Owned, not yet installed. NVIDIA Tesla P4, Pascal architecture (GP104), 16nm. 8GB GDDR5 VRAM, 256-bit bus, 192 GB/s bandwidth. 2560 CUDA cores. Base 886MHz, boost 1114MHz. No display outputs -- compute-only card. Low-profile, passive cooling (no fan -- relies on case airflow). TDP: 75W. Do not treat as current hardware -- install still pending.

VRAM: P4 has 8GB vs P1000's 3.8GB effective -- doubles headroom, enables Q8_0 for 4B models and Q4_K_M for larger models. Bandwidth: P4 at 192 GB/s vs P1000's 82 GB/s -- 2x bandwidth is the main inference speedup driver for small models. CUDA cores: P4 has 2560 vs P1000's 512 -- 5x more cores. Expected tok/s improvement for Hivemind 4B: roughly 1.5-2.5x (bandwidth is the real bottleneck, not core count at these model sizes). # TODO: validate actual tok/s after install

Tesla P4 is roughly equivalent to a GTX 1070 / RX 580 in raw compute terms -- GP104 die (same family as GTX 1070/1080), strong Pascal compute. Closest modern consumer reference: a mid-range card from 2016-2017 era. Better inference value than its age suggests due to 8GB VRAM and 192 GB/s bandwidth -- VRAM and bandwidth matter more than raw TFLOPS for LLM inference on small models.

Tesla P4 is passive cooling -- no fan. Requires adequate case airflow to avoid thermal throttle. P330 Tiny has limited airflow. Use 40mm Noctura fan. Monitor GPU temps after install. No display output -- inference only, not a display card

Validated config for Bobby's stack on P1000. ctx_size: 8192. threads: 12. threads_batch: 12. keep: 96. batch_size: 512. ubatch_size: 256. parallel: 1. n-gpu-layers: 999 (full offload). These are the known-good settings -- do not suggest changes without understanding the thermal and VRAM implications on P330 Tiny hardware.

Setting n-gpu-layers to 999 in llama.cpp means full GPU offload -- all layers go to VRAM. On P1000 4GB this works for Hivemind 4B at Q4_K_M because the model fits. If a model doesn't fit, llama.cpp will split layers between GPU and CPU automatically. When that happens tok/s drops sharply -- PCIe bandwidth between CPU and GPU becomes the bottleneck. Monitor VRAM usage; if spilling, either drop to smaller quant or reduce ctx_size.

Validated context size for Bobby's stack. 8192 tokens balances KV cache VRAM cost against useful context length. KV cache at 8192 for Hivemind 4B Q4_K_M: ~400-600MB VRAM. Increasing ctx_size grows KV cache linearly -- doubling to 16384 would consume ~800-1200MB, potentially pushing P1000 into CPU spill. Vodka CTC exists to make 8192 feel like more by trimming junk before it reaches the context.

AMD Ryzen AI Max+ 395, APU with integrated RDNA 4 GPU. On Bobby's upgrade radar -- not purchased. Key specs relevant to llama-conductor: unified memory architecture (CPU and GPU share the same RAM pool), up to 128GB unified memory at ~256 GB/s bandwidth, ~120W system TDP. Why it matters: eliminates the VRAM bottleneck entirely -- a 14B model at Q4_K_M would fit in unified memory with room to spare. Would unlock Swarm stack at meaningful scale. No purchase decision made -- do not suggest it as an available resource.
